Set

  • Python Set vs Java Set

  • Key Takeaways
  • Interview Tips

Set

Overview

Set is a collection data structure that stores unique elements with no duplicates. It provides efficient membership testing, insertion, and deletion operations.

Key Properties

  • Time Complexity:
    • Add: O(1) average, O(n) worst
    • Remove: O(1) average, O(n) worst
    • Contains: O(1) average, O(n) worst
    • Union/Intersection: O(min(len(s1), len(s2)))
  • Space Complexity: O(n)
  • Core Features: No duplicates, unordered (HashSet), O(1) lookups
  • When to Use: Remove duplicates, membership testing, set operations (union, intersection, difference)

0) Concept

0-1) Types

HashSet

  • Python: set() - unordered, fastest operations
  • Java: HashSet<T> - backed by HashMap
  • Time: O(1) average for add/remove/contains
  • Use case: When order doesn’t matter, need fast lookups

LinkedHashSet

  • Python: No native support (use OrderedDict keys)
  • Java: LinkedHashSet<T> - maintains insertion order
  • Time: O(1) for operations, preserves order
  • Use case: Need set operations + insertion order

TreeSet

  • Python: No native support (use sorted containers)
  • Java: TreeSet<T> - sorted, uses Red-Black tree
  • Time: O(log n) for add/remove/contains
  • Use case: Need sorted elements, range queries

Implementation Comparison

Type Ordering Time Space Use Case
HashSet None O(1) O(n) Fast lookups, no order needed
LinkedHashSet Insertion O(1) O(n) Preserve insertion order
TreeSet Sorted O(log n) O(n) Sorted data, range queries

0-2) Pattern

Pattern 1: Set Operations

1# Union, Intersection, Difference
2s1 = {1, 2, 3}
3s2 = {2, 3, 4}
4
5union = s1 | s2          # {1, 2, 3, 4}
6intersection = s1 & s2   # {2, 3}
7difference = s1 - s2     # {1}
8symmetric_diff = s1 ^ s2 # {1, 4}

Pattern 2: Duplicate Detection

1# Check for duplicates in array
2def has_duplicate(nums):
3    return len(nums) != len(set(nums))
4
5# Find duplicates
6def find_duplicates(nums):
7    seen = set()
8    duplicates = set()
9    for num in nums:
10        if num in seen:
11            duplicates.add(num)
12        seen.add(num)
13    return duplicates

Pattern 3: Two-Set Tracking

1# Track visited and current path (for cycle detection)
2def has_cycle(graph, start):
3    visited = set()
4    current_path = set()
5
6    def dfs(node):
7        if node in current_path:
8            return True  # Cycle detected
9        if node in visited:
10            return False
11
12        visited.add(node)
13        current_path.add(node)
14
15        for neighbor in graph[node]:
16            if dfs(neighbor):
17                return True
18
19        current_path.remove(node)
20        return False
21
22    return dfs(start)

Pattern 4: Set for Path/Ancestry Tracking

1# LC 1650 - Find LCA using set to track ancestors
2def lowestCommonAncestor(p, q):
3    # Track all ancestors of p
4    ancestors = set()
5    while p:
6        ancestors.add(p)
7        p = p.parent
8
9    # Find first common ancestor
10    while q:
11        if q in ancestors:
12            return q
13        q = q.parent
14    return None

1) General form

1-1) Basic OP

1-1-1) Set Creation and Basic Operations

1# Python
2# Create empty set
3s = set()
4s = {}  # Wrong! This creates a dict
5
6# Create with elements
7s = {1, 2, 3}
8s = set([1, 2, 3])
9s = set("abc")  # {'a', 'b', 'c'}
10
11# Add element
12s.add(4)
13
14# Remove element
15s.remove(3)     # Raises KeyError if not exists
16s.discard(3)    # No error if not exists
17s.pop()         # Remove and return arbitrary element
18
19# Check membership
20if 2 in s:
21    print("Found")
22
23# Size
24len(s)
25
26# Clear all
27s.clear()

1// Java
2// Create HashSet
3Set<Integer> set = new HashSet<>();
4
5// Add element
6set.add(1);
7set.add(2);
8set.add(3);
9
10// Remove element
11set.remove(2);
12
13// Check membership
14if (set.contains(1)) {
15    System.out.println("Found");
16}
17
18// Size
19int size = set.size();
20
21// Clear
22set.clear();
23
24// Iterate
25for (int num : set) {
26    System.out.println(num);
27}

1-1-2) Set Operations

1# Python set operations
2s1 = {1, 2, 3, 4}
3s2 = {3, 4, 5, 6}
4
5# Union (elements in either set)
6union1 = s1 | s2
7union2 = s1.union(s2)           # {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}
8
9# Intersection (elements in both sets)
10inter1 = s1 & s2
11inter2 = s1.intersection(s2)    # {3, 4}
12
13# Difference (elements in s1 but not s2)
14diff1 = s1 - s2
15diff2 = s1.difference(s2)       # {1, 2}
16
17# Symmetric difference (elements in either but not both)
18sym1 = s1 ^ s2
19sym2 = s1.symmetric_difference(s2)  # {1, 2, 5, 6}
20
21# Subset check
22is_subset = s1.issubset(s2)     # False
23is_superset = s1.issuperset(s2) # False
24
25# Disjoint check (no common elements)
26is_disjoint = s1.isdisjoint(s2) # False

1// Java set operations
2Set<Integer> s1 = new HashSet<>(Arrays.asList(1, 2, 3, 4));
3Set<Integer> s2 = new HashSet<>(Arrays.asList(3, 4, 5, 6));
4
5// Union
6Set<Integer> union = new HashSet<>(s1);
7union.addAll(s2);  // {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}
8
9// Intersection
10Set<Integer> intersection = new HashSet<>(s1);
11intersection.retainAll(s2);  // {3, 4}
12
13// Difference
14Set<Integer> difference = new HashSet<>(s1);
15difference.removeAll(s2);  // {1, 2}
16
17// Subset check
18boolean isSubset = s2.containsAll(s1);  // false

1-1-3) Converting Between Collections

1# Python conversions
2arr = [1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4]
3
4# Array to set (remove duplicates)
5s = set(arr)  # {1, 2, 3, 4}
6
7# Set to array
8arr_unique = list(s)
9
10# Set to sorted array
11arr_sorted = sorted(s)
12
13# String to set
14char_set = set("hello")  # {'h', 'e', 'l', 'o'}
15
16# Set to string
17s = {'a', 'b', 'c'}
18string = ''.join(sorted(s))  # 'abc'

1// Java conversions
2Integer[] arr = {1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4};
3
4// Array to set
5Set<Integer> set = new HashSet<>(Arrays.asList(arr));
6
7// Set to array
8Integer[] arrUnique = set.toArray(new Integer[0]);
9
10// Set to list
11List<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>(set);
12
13// List to set
14Set<Integer> set2 = new HashSet<>(list);

2) LC Example

2-1) Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Tree III

1# LC 1650. Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Tree III
2# NOTE : there are also dict, recursive.. approaches
3
4# V0''
5# IDEA : set - track ancestry path
6# Time: O(h) where h is tree height
7# Space: O(h) for storing ancestors
8class Solution:
9    def lowestCommonAncestor(self, p, q):
10        # Store all ancestors of p
11        visited = set()
12        while p:
13            visited.add(p)
14            p = p.parent
15
16        # Find first common ancestor with q
17        while q:
18            if q in visited:
19                return q
20            q = q.parent

2-2) Contains Duplicate

1# LC 217. Contains Duplicate
2# V0
3# IDEA: Set to detect duplicates
4class Solution:
5    def containsDuplicate(self, nums):
6        return len(nums) != len(set(nums))
7
8# V0'
9# IDEA: Build set while checking
10class Solution:
11    def containsDuplicate(self, nums):
12        seen = set()
13        for num in nums:
14            if num in seen:
15                return True
16            seen.add(num)
17        return False

1// Java
2// LC 217
3public boolean containsDuplicate(int[] nums) {
4    Set<Integer> seen = new HashSet<>();
5    for (int num : nums) {
6        if (seen.contains(num)) {
7            return true;
8        }
9        seen.add(num);
10    }
11    return false;
12}

2-3) Intersection of Two Arrays

1# LC 349. Intersection of Two Arrays
2# V0
3# IDEA: Set intersection
4class Solution:
5    def intersection(self, nums1, nums2):
6        return list(set(nums1) & set(nums2))
7
8# V0'
9# IDEA: Convert to sets and use intersection
10class Solution:
11    def intersection(self, nums1, nums2):
12        set1 = set(nums1)
13        set2 = set(nums2)
14        return list(set1.intersection(set2))

1// Java
2// LC 349
3public int[] intersection(int[] nums1, int[] nums2) {
4    Set<Integer> set1 = new HashSet<>();
5    for (int num : nums1) {
6        set1.add(num);
7    }
8
9    Set<Integer> result = new HashSet<>();
10    for (int num : nums2) {
11        if (set1.contains(num)) {
12            result.add(num);
13        }
14    }
15
16    return result.stream().mapToInt(i -> i).toArray();
17}

2-4) Happy Number

1# LC 202. Happy Number
2# V0
3# IDEA: Use set to detect cycles
4class Solution:
5    def isHappy(self, n):
6        def get_next(num):
7            total = 0
8            while num > 0:
9                digit = num % 10
10                total += digit * digit
11                num //= 10
12            return total
13
14        seen = set()
15        while n != 1 and n not in seen:
16            seen.add(n)
17            n = get_next(n)
18
19        return n == 1

2-5) Longest Consecutive Sequence

1# LC 128. Longest Consecutive Sequence
2# V0
3# IDEA: Set for O(1) lookups
4# Time: O(n), Space: O(n)
5class Solution:
6    def longestConsecutive(self, nums):
7        if not nums:
8            return 0
9
10        num_set = set(nums)
11        max_length = 0
12
13        for num in num_set:
14            # Only start counting from sequence start
15            if num - 1 not in num_set:
16                current = num
17                length = 1
18
19                while current + 1 in num_set:
20                    current += 1
21                    length += 1
22
23                max_length = max(max_length, length)
24
25        return max_length

1// Java
2// LC 128
3public int longestConsecutive(int[] nums) {
4    Set<Integer> numSet = new HashSet<>();
5    for (int num : nums) {
6        numSet.add(num);
7    }
8
9    int maxLength = 0;
10
11    for (int num : numSet) {
12        // Only start from sequence beginning
13        if (!numSet.contains(num - 1)) {
14            int current = num;
15            int length = 1;
16
17            while (numSet.contains(current + 1)) {
18                current++;
19                length++;
20            }
21
22            maxLength = Math.max(maxLength, length);
23        }
24    }
25
26    return maxLength;
27}

2-6) Single Number

1# LC 136. Single Number
2# V0
3# IDEA: XOR all numbers (duplicates cancel out)
4class Solution:
5    def singleNumber(self, nums):
6        result = 0
7        for num in nums:
8            result ^= num
9        return result
10
11# V0'
12# IDEA: Set addition/removal
13class Solution:
14    def singleNumber(self, nums):
15        return 2 * sum(set(nums)) - sum(nums)

2-7) Valid Sudoku

1# LC 36. Valid Sudoku
2# V0
3# IDEA: Use sets to track seen values
4class Solution:
5    def isValidSudoku(self, board):
6        # Track seen elements in rows, cols, boxes
7        seen = set()
8
9        for i in range(9):
10            for j in range(9):
11                if board[i][j] != '.':
12                    val = board[i][j]
13                    box_idx = (i // 3) * 3 + j // 3
14
15                    # Create unique keys for row, col, box
16                    row_key = f"row_{i}_{val}"
17                    col_key = f"col_{j}_{val}"
18                    box_key = f"box_{box_idx}_{val}"
19
20                    if row_key in seen or col_key in seen or box_key in seen:
21                        return False
22
23                    seen.add(row_key)
24                    seen.add(col_key)
25                    seen.add(box_key)
26
27        return True

2-8) Number of Distinct Islands

1# LC 694. Number of Distinct Islands
2# V0
3# IDEA: Use set to store unique island shapes
4class Solution:
5    def numDistinctIslands(self, grid):
6        if not grid:
7            return 0
8
9        def dfs(i, j, i0, j0):
10            # Record relative position from starting point
11            if (0 <= i < len(grid) and 0 <= j < len(grid[0]) and
12                grid[i][j] == 1):
13                grid[i][j] = 0
14                path.append((i - i0, j - j0))
15                dfs(i+1, j, i0, j0)
16                dfs(i-1, j, i0, j0)
17                dfs(i, j+1, i0, j0)
18                dfs(i, j-1, i0, j0)
19
20        shapes = set()
21        for i in range(len(grid)):
22            for j in range(len(grid[0])):
23                if grid[i][j] == 1:
24                    path = []
25                    dfs(i, j, i, j)
26                    # Convert list to tuple for hashing
27                    shapes.add(tuple(path))
28
29        return len(shapes)

2-9) Linked List Cycle Detection

1# LC 141. Linked List Cycle
2# V0
3# IDEA: Use set to track visited nodes
4class Solution:
5    def hasCycle(self, head):
6        visited = set()
7        current = head
8
9        while current:
10            if current in visited:
11                return True
12            visited.add(current)
13            current = current.next
14
15        return False
16
17# V0'
18# IDEA: Two pointers (Floyd's algorithm) - O(1) space
19class Solution:
20    def hasCycle(self, head):
21        if not head:
22            return False
23
24        slow = head
25        fast = head.next
26
27        while slow != fast:
28            if not fast or not fast.next:
29                return False
30            slow = slow.next
31            fast = fast.next.next
32
33        return True

2-10) Word Pattern

1# LC 290. Word Pattern
2# V0
3# IDEA: Use 2 sets to track bijection
4class Solution:
5    def wordPattern(self, pattern, s):
6        words = s.split()
7
8        if len(pattern) != len(words):
9            return False
10
11        char_to_word = {}
12        word_to_char = {}
13
14        for c, word in zip(pattern, words):
15            if c in char_to_word:
16                if char_to_word[c] != word:
17                    return False
18            else:
19                char_to_word[c] = word
20
21            if word in word_to_char:
22                if word_to_char[word] != c:
23                    return False
24            else:
25                word_to_char[word] = c
26
27        return True

Problem Categories

Category 1: Duplicate Detection (10 problems)

Problem LC # Difficulty Pattern Key Insight
Contains Duplicate 217 Easy Set size len(nums) != len(set(nums))
Contains Duplicate II 219 Easy Sliding window set Keep window of k elements
Contains Duplicate III 220 Medium TreeSet/SortedList Maintain sorted window
Find Duplicate 287 Medium Cycle detection Floyd’s algorithm or set
Find All Duplicates 442 Medium Index marking Use array as hashmap
Single Number 136 Easy XOR/Set XOR cancels duplicates
Single Number II 137 Medium Bit manipulation Count bits mod 3
Single Number III 260 Medium XOR + grouping Group by differing bit
Missing Number 268 Easy Set/XOR Expected vs actual
First Missing Positive 41 Hard In-place set Use array indices

Category 2: Set Operations (8 problems)

Problem LC # Difficulty Pattern Key Insight
Intersection of Two Arrays 349 Easy Set intersection set1 & set2
Intersection of Two Arrays II 350 Easy Counter Track frequencies
Union of Two Arrays - Easy Set union set1
Distribute Candies 575 Easy Set size min(len(set), n/2)
Uncommon Words 884 Easy Set difference Count once in either
Set Mismatch 645 Easy Set difference Find duplicate & missing
Fair Candy Swap 888 Easy Set membership Target difference
Buddy Strings 859 Easy Set of pairs Check swap possible

Category 3: Path/Ancestry Tracking (6 problems)

Problem LC # Difficulty Pattern Key Insight
Lowest Common Ancestor III 1650 Medium Ancestor set Track parent path
Linked List Cycle 141 Easy Visited set Two pointers better
Linked List Cycle II 142 Medium Visited set Floyd’s algorithm
Course Schedule 207 Medium DFS + set Detect cycle
Course Schedule II 210 Medium Topological sort Track visited/path
Find Eventual Safe Nodes 802 Medium DFS + states Terminal vs unsafe

Category 4: Sequence Problems (7 problems)

Problem LC # Difficulty Pattern Key Insight
Longest Consecutive Sequence 128 Medium Set lookups Start from sequence begin
Longest Substring Without Repeat 3 Medium Sliding window set Track seen chars
Longest Palindrome 409 Easy Char frequency Pairs + one odd
Maximum Length of Repeated Subarray 718 Medium Set of tuples Rolling hash
Arithmetic Slices 413 Medium Set of differences Track valid sequences
Happy Number 202 Easy Cycle detection Track seen sums
Valid Sudoku 36 Medium Multiple sets Row/col/box tracking

Category 5: Graph/Island Problems (5 problems)

Problem LC # Difficulty Pattern Key Insight
Number of Islands 200 Medium DFS/BFS visited Track processed cells
Number of Distinct Islands 694 Medium Shape hashing Normalize positions
Max Area of Island 695 Medium DFS + visited Track seen cells
Island Perimeter 463 Easy Border counting Count land-water edges
Surrounded Regions 130 Medium Border DFS Mark connected to border

Category 6: String/Pattern Matching (6 problems)

Problem LC # Difficulty Pattern Key Insight
Isomorphic Strings 205 Easy Bijection Two maps or sets
Word Pattern 290 Easy Bijection Char ↔ word mapping
Group Anagrams 49 Medium Sorted key Use sorted string
Find Anagrams 438 Medium Window + counter Sliding character counts
Jewels and Stones 771 Easy Set membership Set of jewels
Unique Email Addresses 929 Easy Normalize + set Clean emails

Decision Framework

When to Use Set vs Other Data Structures

1Problem Analysis:
2
31. Need to track unique elements?
4   ├── YES → Consider Set
5   │   ├── Need ordering?
6   │   │   ├── YES → TreeSet (Java) / sorted list (Python)
7   │   │   └── NO → HashSet
8   │   ├── Need count?
9   │   │   └── NO → Use Counter/HashMap instead
10   │   └── Need fast lookups?
11   │       └── YES → HashSet (O(1) average)
12   └── NO → Consider other structures
13
142. Performing set operations (union, intersection)?
15   ├── YES → Use Set
16   │   └── Multiple operations → Build set once
17   └── NO → Continue analysis
18
193. Detecting duplicates/cycles?
20   ├── YES → Use Set for visited tracking
21   │   ├── Space constrained?
22   │   │   └── YES → Consider Floyd's algorithm
23   │   └── NO → Set is ideal
24   └── NO → Continue analysis
25
264. Checking membership repeatedly?
27   ├── YES → Convert to Set first
28   │   └── O(n) conversion + O(1) lookups
29   └── NO → Linear search may be fine

Set vs HashMap Choice

Use Set When Use HashMap When
Only need existence check Need key-value mapping
Removing duplicates Counting frequencies
Set operations (∪, ∩, -) Need associated data
Memory efficient (no values) Need to track counts/indices

Python Set vs Java Set

Feature Python set Java HashSet
Creation s = {1,2,3} or set() Set<T> s = new HashSet<>()
Add s.add(x) s.add(x)
Remove s.remove(x) / s.discard(x) s.remove(x)
Contains x in s s.contains(x)
Union `s1 s2ors1.union(s2)`
Intersection s1 & s2 or s1.intersection(s2) s1.retainAll(s2)
Difference s1 - s2 or s1.difference(s2) s1.removeAll(s2)
Size len(s) s.size()
Empty check not s or len(s) == 0 s.isEmpty()

Summary & Best Practices

Key Takeaways

  1. When to Use Set:

    • Remove duplicates from collection
    • Fast membership testing (O(1) average)
    • Performing set operations (union, intersection, difference)
    • Tracking visited nodes in graphs/trees
    • Detecting cycles

  2. Performance Characteristics:

    • HashSet: O(1) average, O(n) worst (hash collisions)
    • TreeSet: O(log n) for all operations
    • LinkedHashSet: O(1) operations + insertion order

  3. Common Patterns:

    • Convert array to set to remove duplicates
    • Use set for O(1) lookups instead of O(n) linear search
    • Track visited nodes with set
    • Detect cycles by checking if element already in set

  4. Space-Time Tradeoffs:

    • Set uses O(n) extra space for O(1) operations
    • Consider two-pointer techniques if space is constrained
    • For small inputs, linear search may be faster

Interview Tips

Common Mistakes to Avoid:

  • Using {} to create empty set in Python (creates dict instead)
  • Forgetting that sets are unordered (don’t assume order)
  • Not considering TreeSet when you need sorted elements
  • Using set when you need to count occurrences (use Counter/HashMap)

Optimization Tips:

  • Convert lists to sets before repeated membership checks
  • Use set operations instead of manual loops
  • Consider frozenset for immutable/hashable sets
  • Use set comprehensions for cleaner code

Follow-up Questions:

  • “Can you solve it with O(1) space?” → Consider Floyd’s algorithm
  • “What if we need to preserve order?” → LinkedHashSet or OrderedDict
  • “What if we need sorted elements?” → TreeSet or sorted list
  • “What about duplicates with different data?” → Use HashMap instead
← Back to Cheat Sheets Edit on GitHub →
← Segment TreeShortest Path Comparison →
